reasoning: Provide a concise, neutral summary of the article’s key facts and positions regarding the Yomper statue and the consultation process.
output: The Yomper statue, created by Philip Jackson and depicting a Royal Marine marching during the 1982 Falklands War, currently stands outside the Royal Marines Museum at Eastney and was unveiled by former prime minister Lady Thatcher in 1992. The National Museum of the Royal Navy (NMRN) is planning to relocate the Royal Marines Museum from Eastney to Portsmouth Historic Dockyard as part of a £13m lottery-funded project and wants to move the statue with it. Campaigners argue the statue is a local memorial to the Falklands War and should remain at Eastney, with an online petition attracting more than 1,800 signatures. Sheila Mackie, who started the petition, said the statue’s scale and its portrayal of the campaign can be appreciated only in a suitable space, not indoors. Conservative-led Portsmouth City Council expressed a clear preference that the statue stay at Eastney, noting it has graced the seafront for years and is tied to the Marines’ history there. NMRN director Jon Rawlinson said the new site could attract about 750,000 visitors annually, compared with 40,000 at Eastney, and that while more people would see it at the historic dockyard, the statue was built for Eastney and is part of Eastney. He stressed that no decision has been made and all public consultation comments will be considered.
